BRANNETH'S STORY (maybe a relief lol, maybe also a bit long??)

She never expected anything; she couldn’t. Expecting something had never been part of her reasoning. Expecting hatred, love, or even a simple hug from her mother. From an early age, she understood that expecting was for those who were not her, who did not have her skin, her eyes, her body, or her soul.

Others expected things; she only acted. Because otherwise, her father would be quieter, her mother would be angrier. She never questioned the reason for things; she didn’t understand them. She only understood the “how”. How to keep her mother from getting angry, how to stay silent, how to plow the earth and plant seeds so that her father might look at her once if possible.

By the age of five, before she could even write her own name, Branneth already understood why no one looked at her, how everyone avoided her gaze, her touch, her very existence. She constantly reminded herself that she was not worthy of expecting anything. She always searched for the how to change, how to be prettier for the village elders, how to smile so that the other girls would want to play with her.

In the end, even though desire and the how were present, they brought no results. So, betrayed by her own mind, Branneth waited. She waited to be looked at, even once. But her reality was different. Not even her father looked at her face. Even less did her mother look into her eyes that autumn afternoon when she hit her. From that moment, Branneth thought her eyes were ugly.

At the age of 20, she experienced an impact on her life and soul that she had never thought possible in her small, rough mind. She discovered the forge. It was the first time Branneth truly enjoyed heat, the first time she enjoyed the resounding noise of metal against flesh being shaped by the hammer. That spring morning, she realized that her large hands were capable not only of destruction and bringing hardship.

She could perform the how to solve things more easily—without noise, without shouting—just her and the forge.

One winter night, her father died. Branneth did not cry—she never did—but she did feel bad. Bad for not being the girl with the pretty smile her father had expected. Bad because that cold night, that distant man she called father, had looked at her. He had looked at Branneth after 21 years. Then that man... he cried. He cried with sadness, with heaviness. Branneth said nothing. She watched her father, watched his heavy body releasing a final exhalation of forgiveness. That was the closest act to forgiveness she had ever experienced in her life.

Then she saw black. She didn’t remember how many days or months passed—she only saw red, a knife, the black slowly flooding her field of vision, and in a distant tunnel, her mother and she looked at each other.

She was angry. He cried. No one had ever hugged Branneth.
